coredata.dc:title: Red emissive carbon dots-based probe for rapid identification and continuous tracking of Gram-positive bacteria in tumor cells
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Zhengqi
Zhu, Haimei
Peng, Nannan
Song, Jian
Sun, Rongjun
Wang, Jingmin
Zhu, Fenfang
Wang, Yongzhong
coredata.dc:description: Red fluorescent carbon dots (R-CDs) and blue fluorescent carbon dots (B-CDs) were synthesized, and then coupled with D-alanine (D-Ala) to construct CDs based probes (R-CDs@D-Ala, B-CDs@D-Ala). The two probes can not only rapidly and selectively identify Gram-positive bacteria within 30 s, but also keep continuous tracking for 24 h. Further, R-CDs@D-Ala was successfully used to detect G+ bacteria from Hela cells, showing good identification results. Moreover, the fluorescence superposition of the two probes can display the differences in the growth and division status of different G+ bacteria, providing strong support for bacterial diagnosis and treatment in the tumor microenvironment. Therefore, CDs based nanoprobes provide a rapid and effective method for diagnosis of bacterial infectious diseases and has good practical application value.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Red fluorescent carbon dots
Gram-positive bacteria
Selective identification
Continuous tracking
Tumor microbial testing

All co–authors acknowledge the support of the Key Laboratory of Human Microenvironment and Precision Medicine of Anhui Higher Education Institutes, Anhui University.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Bacterial imaging incubated with samples. (b) 3D scanning of mixed bacteria (S. aureus and E. coli) treated with R-CDs@D-Ala. (c) Mixed bacteria imaging incubated with R-CDs@D-Ala. (d) Flow cytometry analyzed the targeting of bacteria.
(a) Monitoring the growth status of S.aureus by probes. (b) S. aureus and B. paralycetes imaging by probes. (c) Double-staining experiment.

head.ce:title: Red emissive carbon dots-based probe for rapid identification and continuous tracking of Gram-positive bacteria in tumor cells

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Life Sciences, Anhui University, Hefei 230601, Anhui, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Life Sciences, Anhui University, Hefei 230601, Anhui, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Red fluorescent carbon dots (R-CDs) and blue fluorescent carbon dots (B-CDs) were synthesized, and then coupled with D-alanine (D-Ala) to construct CDs based probes (R-CDs@D-Ala, B-CDs@D-Ala). The two probes can not only rapidly and selectively identify Gram-positive bacteria within 30 s, but also keep continuous tracking for 24 h. Further, R-CDs@D-Ala was successfully used to detect G+ bacteria from Hela cells, showing good identification results. Moreover, the fluorescence superposition of the two probes can display the differences in the growth and division status of different G+ bacteria, providing strong support for bacterial diagnosis and treatment in the tumor microenvironment. Therefore, CDs based nanoprobes provide a rapid and effective method for diagnosis of bacterial infectious diseases and has good practical application value.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Gram-positive bacteria, as a major member of bacteria, has strong vitality and various growth/division methods [1–3]. Diseases (skin infections [4], pneumonia [5], sepsis [6] and other diseases) caused by G+ bacteria may lead to organ damage and even death. To date, many traditional methods, including Gram staining [7] and polymerase chain reaction [8], have been widely used to identify G+ bacteria. Unfortunately, these methods require special equipment and professional operators, and are time-consuming, laborious, costly, which limit the application in the detection of bacteria in actual samples. The serious threat of COVID-19 makes the development of microbial detection technology imminent [9–10]. Fluorescence analysis technology has rapidly developed into one of the main methods of bacterial detection, benefiting from its convenient use and high resolution. However, the commonly used fluorescent materials are mainly fluorescent proteins [11] (high cost and short activity time) and fluorescent dyes [12] (high toxicity and photobleaching), which greatly limit the further application of fluorescence technology.

As a new generation of fluorescent nanomaterials, the development of carbon dots (CDs) has injected new vitality into the application of fluorescence detection technology, which is a kind of “core-shell” typed nanomaterials with a diameter of ≦ 10 nm, composed of sp 2 conjugated carbon cores embedded in sp 3 hybridized carbon matrices and organic functional groups or polymers as shells [13–15]. Compared with fluorescent protein, CDs has the characteristics of low cost, good stability, strong anti-bleaching ability [13–14], while CDs over fluorescent dyes are biocompatibility and no complicated steps [16]. Consequently, CDs has gradually attracted extensive attention as an alternative fluorescent agent in bacteria imaging and detection [17–18]. However, the fluorescence of CDs in the blue-green wavelength range is interfered by the fluorescence of the experimental background in vivo. Therefore, more and more researchers are committed to the development of long wavelength fluorescent CDs [19–20].

In this work, R-CDs and B-CDs were developed and then coupled with D-alanine to construct fluorescent probes (R-CDs@D-Ala, B-CDs@D-Ala) that can specifically target G+ bacteria (Scheme S1). Surprisingly, the probe of 200 μg/ml can quickly identify live G+ bacteria within 30 s without any decoloration steps, and the fluorescence was stable within 24 h without affecting the growth of bacteria. More importantly, it can be clearly observed that the presence of G+ bacteria around the tumor cells through the double-stain localization of the two probes. Moreover, the probes can distinguish the differences in the division status of two G+ bacteria by fluorescence superposition. The excellent properties of CDs-based probe will overcome the shortcomings of current bacterial detection in complex physiological environments, and help it play a great role in diagnosing infection and other biological applications.

R-CDs (B-CDs) were synthesized via solvothermal treatment of o-phenylenediamine and phenylalanine (Scheme S1). Briefly, 1.08 g o-phenylenediamine and 3.30 g phenylalanine was mixed thoroughly in 70 mL DMF/H2O (H2O). This mixture was then heated at 200 °C for 12 h. After cooling to room temperature naturally, the brown solution was filtered through a 0.22 µm filter membrane several times and then dialyzed (MWCO of 500) for 48 h in ultrapure water to remove the residual reactants. Finally, the dialyzed solution was freeze–dried at −60 °C under vacuum to obtain a milky white powder.

TEM image (Fig. S1) present that the R-CDs are well dispersed and uniform in size of 1.70 nm with a spherical like structure. And the HRTEM image depicted that the R-CDs have high crystallinity with clear lattice fringes of about 0.19 nm, corresponding to the (104) facet of graphite carbon [17]. Similarly, the average particle size of B-CDs is about 2.87 nm (Fig. S2), larger than that of R-CDs, which may result in the difference in optical properties. HRTEM of B-CDs showed well-resolved lattice fringes with a lattice distance of 0.21 nm, which is close to the (100) facet of graphite carbon [17].

The conjugation of D-Ala on R-CDs was confirmed by the appearance of a stretching vibration at 1690 cm−1, 3380 cm−1 in the FT-TR spectra (Fig. S3a) [18,21,22] and the surface groups (O-H, C = O, C = C and-COOH) of R-CDs@D-Ala at 3230, 1639, 1592 and 1385 cm−1 are similar to that of R-CDs, respectively. Similarly, Fig. S4a demonstrated the successful construction of B-CDs@D-Ala. As shown in Fig. S3b, the XPS spectrum of R-CDs@D-Ala explicitly showed the co-existence of C, N and O elements with content of 69.7At.%, 18.28 At.% and 12.0 At.%, respectively. Among them, the content of C and N is higher than that of R-CDs (79.4 and 13.2 At.%, respectively), probably resulted from the incorporation of D-Ala, which is in accordance with the result from FT-IR. The high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1 s, N1s and O 1 s regions (Figs. S3–S5) exhibited that there is no groups are added on CDs after the introduction of D-Ala [23], which only causes the change of functional group content.

R-CDs possessed a broad absorption from UV to red light region (Fig. S6a), and the absorption peak at 500 nm can be attributed to the inclusion of C = C in R-CDs [20]. Moreover, Fig. S6b displayed the optimal excitation wavelength of R-CDs is 470 nm (emission at 610 nm), while that of B-CDs is 400 nm (emission at 470 nm) from Fig. S7. The reason for this difference may be the change of optical properties caused by size. Additionally, Fig. S8 showed that the absolute fluorescence quantum yields of R-CDs is 9.15%, which is comparable with those of CDs reported in other literatures [24–25]. Further, compared with the unmodified CDs, amino acid modification is conducive to the fluorescence intensity of CDs (Figs. S6c and S7c).

As depicted in Fig. 1 a, R-CDs can act as a potential bacterial imaging agent to stably image bacteria within 24 h. Fig. S9a proved the similar results. Notably, R-CDs@D-Ala can make S. aureus produce obvious red fluorescence, while E. coli hardly produce fluorescence, showing excellent G+ bacterial targeting. The same results (Fig. 1b) from 3D model constructed by high-resolution laser confocal further verifyed this discovery. The evidence of time-dependent fluorescence images (Fig. 1c) obtained by the probe staining mixed bacteria demonstrated that R-CDs@D-Ala can rapidly image the S. aureus within 30 s, and the imaging effect becomes better with the extension of culture time, while that of E. coli is hardly enhanced. The same results were also comfirmed in Fig. S9. Meanwhile, the flow cytometry analysis (Fig. 1d) also fully proved that R-CDs@D-Ala have good selectivity for S. aureus, under the same conditions.

The fluorescence of bacteria co-cultured with R-CDs@D-Ala increased with the increase of culture time and OD value at 37 °C (R2 = 0.91, Fig. 2 a), the fluorescence intensity of bacteria decreased with the decrease of culture temperature (R2 = 0.99521), demonstrating R-CDs@D-Ala has ability to monitor the growth status of G+ bacteria. Further, the results in Fig. 2b showed that R-CDs@D-Ala can distinguish G+ bacteria in different splitting states. S. aureus and B. paralycetes are bifidoated within 30 s, where S. aureus forms a “cell plate” in the middle and divides into two, while B. paralytica begins to divide the head and forms two independent bacteria.

A tumor microbial model was constructed to evaluate the practical application of the probes for detecting G+ bacteria at the cell level. Fig. 2c exhibited that R-CDs@D-Ala remain well targeting effect in the cancer cell microenvironment, and the double staining experiment of two probes verified that S. aureus destroyed the membrane structure of Hela cells. Thus, R-CDs@D-Ala is a promising probe to distinguish G+ bacteria in clinical diagnosis.

R-CDs and B-CDs with excellent properties were prepared, and then combined with D-Ala to obtain CDs based fluorescent probes capable of targeting G+ bacteria. The R-CDs@D-Ala can rapidly image G+ bacteria within 30 s and maintain stability for 24 h. The fluorescence intensity of G+ bacteria co-cultured with R-CDs@D-Ala was positively correlated with their growth state. Meanwhile, R-CDs@D-Ala and B-CDs@D-Ala can distinguish the division state of different G+ bacteria. Moreover, the double-staining experiment further confirming the application potential of the probe in tumor microbial detection. Therefore, the successful construction of CDs based fluorescent probes provides strong support for the screening and prevention of microbial pathogens.

Zhengqi Zhang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis. Haimei Zhu: Investigation, Formal analysis. Nannan Peng: . Jian Song: Investigation, Formal analysis. Rongjun Sun: Investigation, Formal analysis. Jingmin Wang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Fenfang Zhu: Validation, Formal analysis. Yongzhong Wang: Conceptualization, Project administration, Resources, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was financed by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (YZ Wang, No. 31770066, 31470218) and the Natural Science Foundation of the Anhui Higher Education Institutions of China (JM Wang, No. KJ2021A0051). All co–authors acknowledge the support of the Key Laboratory of Human Microenvironment and Precision Medicine of Anhui Higher Education Institutes, Anhui University.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matlet.2023.134233.
